section.title: The Conquest of Bashan
section.sectionRationale: This closes Moses' historical review of the wilderness journey with the second great Transjordan victory, over Og of Bashan, the allotment of that land, and Moses' own poignant, personal exclusion from the land he has led Israel toward for forty years.
section.thesis: As Israel completes the conquest of the Transjordan by defeating Og, the last of the giant kings, Moses allots that land to two and a half tribes on condition that they first help their brothers conquer the land west of the Jordan, then pleads with the LORD to cross over himself and is refused, though he is granted a view of the land and commissions Joshua as his successor.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the defeat of Og king of Bashan; the land taken and divided; instructions to the two and a half tribes; Moses commissions Joshua; and Moses' plea, met by the LORD's firm but merciful refusal.

units.label: The defeat of Og king of Bashan

verses.text: Then we turned and went up the road to Bashan, and Og king of Bashan and his whole army came out to meet us in battle at Edrei.
But the LORD said to me, “Do not fear him, for I have delivered him into your hand, along with all his people and his land. Do to him as you did to Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived in Heshbon.”
So the LORD our God also delivered Og king of Bashan and his whole army into our hands. We struck them down until no survivor was left.
At that time we captured all sixty of his cities. There was not a single city we failed to take, the entire region of Argob, the kingdom of Og in Bashan.
All these cities were fortified with high walls and gates and bars, and there were many more unwalled villages.
We devoted them to destruction, as we had done to Sihon king of Heshbon, utterly destroying the men, women, and children of every city.
But all the livestock and plunder of the cities we carried off for ourselves.
At that time we took from the two kings of the Amorites the land across the Jordan, from the Arnon Valley as far as Mount Hermon,
which the Sidonians call Sirion but the Amorites call Senir,
all the cities of the plateau, all of Gilead, and all of Bashan as far as the cities of Salecah and Edrei in the kingdom of Og.
(For only Og king of Bashan had remained of the remnant of the Rephaim. His bed of iron, nine cubits long and four cubits wide, is still in Rabbah of the Ammonites.)
So at that time we took possession of this land. To the Reubenites and Gadites I gave the land beyond Aroer along the Arnon Valley, and half the hill country of Gilead, along with its cities.
To the half-tribe of Manasseh I gave the rest of Gilead and all of Bashan, the kingdom of Og. (The entire region of Argob, the whole territory of Bashan, used to be called the land of the Rephaim.)

Jair, a descendant of Manasseh, took the whole region of Argob as far as the border of the Geshurites and Maacathites. He renamed Bashan after himself, Havvoth-jair, by which it is called to this day.
To Machir I gave Gilead,
and to the Reubenites and Gadites I gave the territory from Gilead to the Arnon Valley (the middle of the valley was the border) and up to the Jabbok River, the border of the Ammonites.
The Jordan River in the Arabah bordered it from Chinnereth to the Sea of the Arabah (the Salt Sea) with the slopes of Pisgah to the east.

units.label: Instructions to the two and a half tribes

verses.text: At that time I commanded you: “The LORD your God has given you this land to possess. All your men of valor are to cross over, armed for battle, ahead of your brothers, the Israelites.
But your wives, your children, and your livestock, I know that you have much livestock, may remain in the cities I have given you,

until the LORD gives rest to your brothers as He has to you, and they too have taken possession of the land that the LORD your God is giving them across the Jordan. Then each of you may return to the {{nachalah|possession}} I have given you.”

units.label: Moses commissions Joshua
units.note: the LORD Himself will fight for you

verses.text: And at that time I commanded Joshua: “Your own eyes have seen all that the LORD your God has done to these two kings. The LORD will do the same to all the kingdoms you are about to enter.
Do not be afraid of them, for the LORD your God Himself will fight for you.”

units.label: Moses' plea, and the LORD's refusal
units.note: enough, do not speak to Me again

verses.text: At that time I also {{chanan|pleaded}} with the LORD:
“O Lord GOD, You have begun to show Your greatness and power to Your servant. For what god in heaven or on earth can perform such works and mighty acts as Yours?
Please let me cross over and see the good land beyond the Jordan, that pleasant hill country as well as Lebanon!”
But the LORD was angry with me on account of you, and He would not listen to me. “That is enough,” the LORD said to me. “Do not speak to Me again about this matter.
Go to the top of Pisgah and look to the west and north and south and east. See the land with your own eyes, for you will not cross this Jordan.
But commission Joshua, encourage him, and strengthen him, for he will cross over ahead of the people and enable them to inherit the land that you will see.”
So we stayed in the valley opposite Beth-peor.

chanan.gloss: To seek favor, to plead earnestly for grace; the root behind the Hebrew word for grace, chen. Moses does not demand entry to the land as a right but pleads for it as an undeserved favor, and even this plea is refused.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. Used of the land given to each tribe, it points to land as a gift held in trust from the LORD rather than property earned or seized by human effort alone.

basic.context: Moses finishes the Transjordan conquest narrative with the defeat of Og, the giant king of Bashan, the division of that land among two and a half tribes, and his own deeply personal plea to enter the promised land, which the LORD firmly refuses even as He shows Moses mercy in other ways.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history retold with theological weight: the defeat of a legendary giant-king shows the LORD's power, while Moses' refused request shows that even history's greatest servant of God does not escape the consequences of his own sin, yet is still treated with tenderness by God.
basic.originalLanguages: Moses says he chanan, pleaded or sought favor, with the LORD, using the very word behind chen, grace, showing he asked for an undeserved gift rather than a right owed him. The land given to the tribes is their nachalah, inheritance, a gift held in trust from God rather than territory simply seized by conquest.
basic.typology: Moses, the great mediator who leads Israel to the very border of the land but cannot enter it himself, anticipates the need for a greater mediator who both leads His people fully into their inheritance and shares fully in it with them, fulfilled in Jesus, the true and greater Joshua.
basic.christ: Moses' exclusion from the land, despite his faithful service, points to the insufficiency of even the greatest human mediator; Hebrews 3:1-6 contrasts Moses as a faithful servant in God's house with Christ as the faithful Son who builds and inherits the house itself.
basic.god: God is powerful enough to defeat a legendary giant-king in a single battle, yet He is also firm and personal enough to say no to His most faithful servant's heartfelt plea, while still showing Moses real kindness by letting him see the land and by entrusting Joshua to his personal encouragement.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 21:33-35 gives the parallel account of Og's defeat. Hebrews 3:1-6 contrasts Moses the servant with Christ the Son. Deuteronomy 34:1-4 records Moses actually viewing the land from Pisgah as promised here.
basic.oneStory: The God who defeats giants and apportions inheritances is the same God who lovingly, firmly says no to His faithful servant Moses, revealing that even the greatest human leader cannot bring God's people fully into their rest. That work waits for a greater mediator, Jesus, who leads His people all the way home.

context.literary: This chapter closes the historical retrospective begun in chapter one, moving from the second great military victory to administrative details of land division, and ending with the personal, almost intimate account of Moses' own request and its refusal.
context.historical: Bashan's fertile plateau and Og's legendary size made this a memorable victory for Israel; the detail about his iron bed preserved in Rabbah shows this was a well-known, verifiable historical memory for Moses' original audience.
context.canonical: Moses' exclusion from the land becomes a recurring theme through the rest of Deuteronomy and is picked up in the New Testament as an image of the law's inability to bring people fully into God's promised rest, a task reserved for Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the completion of the Transjordan conquest, to establish the terms under which the eastern tribes would receive their inheritance, and to explain honestly why Moses himself, despite his lifetime of faithful service, would not enter the land.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes specific historical events, a particular military campaign, a particular land division, and Moses' particular plea and its answer. It prescribes no ongoing practice for later Israel; its abiding lesson concerns God's sovereignty, mercy, and the seriousness of sin even in a leader as faithful as Moses.

debates.question: Was God's refusal of Moses' request harsh or merciful?

views.label: It appears severe given Moses' lifetime of faithful service
views.case: Moses had led Israel faithfully for forty years through immense hardship, and his single failure at Meribah seems small compared to the people's repeated, larger rebellions, which makes the refusal feel disproportionate.

views.label: It is a serious but genuinely merciful judgment
views.case: God still allows Moses to see the entire land from Pisgah, entrusts him with commissioning Joshua, and later even brings him back into the land in the transfiguration, Matthew 17:3, showing real tenderness within a real and deserved consequence.

debates.bearing: Both views take seriously that leaders are held to a high standard, Numbers 20:12, James 3:1, while affirming that God's discipline of Moses was woven through with real kindness, not mere severity.

study.originalLanguages: Moses uses chanan, to plead for favor, the root of chen, grace, showing he approached God not on the basis of merit but as a suppliant seeking an undeserved gift. The land given to the tribes is called their nachalah, an inheritance held in trust from the LORD, not simply property won by the sword.

typology.type: Moses, the faithful mediator excluded from the land
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the need for a greater mediator who both leads and shares fully in His people's inheritance, fulfilled in Christ, Hebrews 3:1-6.

typology.type: The defeat of Og, the last of the Rephaim giants
typology.fulfillment: A foretaste of the LORD's total triumph over every seemingly invincible power, completed in Christ, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: Moses, faithful in all God's house as a servant, is contrasted in Hebrews 3:1-6 with Christ, faithful as a Son over God's house; Moses can bring Israel to the border of rest but not into it, while Christ actually brings His people into the promised, eternal rest that Canaan could only foreshadow. Moses' exclusion, despite his righteousness, underscores that no human mediator, however faithful, can perfect what only Christ fulfills.
study.god: God is powerful enough to overthrow a legendary giant-king in a single decisive battle, and He is also personally engaged enough to hear, weigh, and ultimately and firmly refuse His most faithful servant's heartfelt request, while still tempering that refusal with real kindness, a view of the land and a meaningful task in commissioning Joshua.

crossReferences.note: The parallel narrative account of Israel's defeat of Og.
Contrasts Moses, faithful as a servant, with Christ, faithful as the Son who builds and inherits the house.
Records Moses actually viewing the land from Pisgah, exactly as promised here.
Moses appears with Christ on the mount of transfiguration, within sight of the very land he was once denied.

study.oneStory: The God who defeats even legendary giants and fairly apportions every tribe's inheritance is the same God who lovingly but firmly says no to His faithful servant Moses, showing that no human leader, however devoted, can finish the work of bringing God's people fully into their rest. That greater work belongs to Jesus, the true and better mediator, who leads His people all the way home.

quiz.q: What was distinctive about Og's bed, mentioned as evidence of his origin (v.11)?

options.text: It was made of iron, nine cubits long and four cubits wide, a relic of his descent from the Rephaim giants.
options.note: Verse 11 gives this exact detail as proof of Og's remarkable size and ancestry.

options.text: It was made of pure gold.
options.note: The text specifies iron, not gold.

options.text: Nothing distinctive about it is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 11 gives specific, memorable details about it.

options.text: It was the same size as an ordinary bed.
options.note: Its enormous size, nine by four cubits, is exactly the point being made.

quiz.q: What condition did Moses place on the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh for receiving their land east of the Jordan (vv.18-20)?

options.text: Their men of valor had to cross over armed and help their brothers conquer the land west of the Jordan first.
options.note: Verses 18-20 state this condition clearly.

options.text: They had to give up all their livestock.
options.note: Verse 19 explicitly allows their livestock to remain with their families; nothing is confiscated.

options.text: They had to convert their cities into walled fortresses.
options.note: No such building requirement is mentioned here.

options.text: No condition was placed on them at all.
options.note: A clear condition, fighting alongside their brothers first, is stated in verses 18-20.

quiz.q: How did the LORD respond to Moses' plea to cross over into the good land (vv.26-27)?

options.text: He refused, telling Moses to speak no more of it, but let him view the land from Pisgah.
options.note: Verses 26-27 record exactly this firm refusal paired with a real concession.

options.text: He granted the request immediately.
options.note: The text records a clear refusal, not a granted request.

options.text: He postponed the decision indefinitely.
options.note: The refusal in verse 26 is final and immediate, not postponed.

options.text: He gave no response at all.
options.note: Verses 26-27 record a detailed, specific response.

quiz.q: What instruction did Moses give directly to Joshua in this chapter (vv.21-22, 28)?

options.text: Do not be afraid, for the LORD Himself will fight for you; be encouraged and strengthened to lead the people across.
options.note: Verses 21-22 and 28 record this direct commissioning and encouragement.

options.text: Wait several more years before leading the people.
options.note: No delay is instructed; Joshua is commissioned to lead immediately when the time comes.

options.text: Appoint someone else in his place.
options.note: The opposite occurs; Joshua himself is directly commissioned.

options.text: No instruction to Joshua is recorded in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 21-22 and 28 record specific instructions given directly to him.

quiz.q: Why was Moses barred from entering the land, according to his own words here (v.26)?

options.text: The LORD was angry with him on account of the people, and would not listen to his plea.
options.note: Verse 26 states this directly in Moses' own retelling.

options.text: Moses had committed idolatry.
options.note: No such charge is made here or elsewhere against Moses.

options.text: Moses refused to lead the people any further.
options.note: The opposite is true; Moses actively pleaded to continue leading them into the land.

options.text: No reason at all is given here.
options.note: Verse 26 gives a specific, if summarized, reason.

questions: Moses pleaded earnestly with God, yet God said, 'Enough, speak no more of this.' How do you respond when God says no to something you have pleaded for with everything in you?

Even though Moses could not enter the land himself, he poured his remaining strength into commissioning and strengthening Joshua. Who are you investing in and strengthening for a future you may not personally see fulfilled?

The tribes who settled east of the Jordan still had to fight for their brothers before enjoying their own rest. Where is God calling you to serve someone else's need before settling into your own comfort?
Moses was allowed to see the land even though he could not enter it. What are the 'Pisgah views', the partial glimpses of things not yet fully realized, that God has given you?

Og's massive iron bed testified to real, imposing power, yet he still fell before the LORD in a single battle. What imposing obstacle in your life needs to be measured against God's power rather than its own size?

translationNotes.note: The note that Sidonians and Amorites called Mount Hermon by different names, Sirion and Senir, reflects Moses' careful, almost geographer's precision in this historical review.

Og's 'bed of iron' may describe an iron-framed bed or, as some scholars suggest, a black basalt sarcophagus; either reading supports the same point, that Og was an unusually large, formidable figure whose defeat displayed the LORD's power.

'That is enough' translates a short, final Hebrew phrase (rav lakh) that closes off further appeal, showing the LORD's refusal was firm and complete even as He immediately offers Moses a real, if lesser, kindness in verse 27.
